text: I just put up a video about PANE, a summer project which I presented at the LIVE workshop a few weeks ago: <http://joshuahhh.com/projects/pane/>. It's a prototype live, functional programming environment centered around data-visibility – all intermediate values are visible by default, and you construct a program by acting on these values. Please take a look, and let me know if you have any thoughts.

I just put up a video about PANE, a summer project which I presented at the LIVE workshop a few weeks ago: <http://joshuahhh.com/projects/pane/>. It's a prototype live, functional programming environment centered around data-visibility – all intermediate values are visible by default, and you construct a program by acting on these values. Please take a look, and let me know if you have any thoughts.

Hey! UI looks quite pleasant, which is rare for prototype programming tools :smiley: I like fonts, spacing and colors very much.

I see that this prototype mostly works with pure functions. Do you plan to expand this idea to cover IO? It feels like it gonna require completely different interface.

Hey! UI looks quite pleasant, which is rare for prototype programming tools :smiley: I like fonts, spacing and colors very much.

I see that this prototype mostly works with pure functions. Do you plan to expand this idea to cover IO? It feels like it gonna require completely different interface.

<@UCKRZS3DZ> Thanks! The visual design is certainly far from ideal, but I tried to follow basic principles.

As for pure/non-pure functions – PANE is for defining the data-processing parts of a system, so IO isn't in scope. I've been told I'm talking about the "Functional Core" part of the "Functional Core, Imperative Shell" distinction, which sounds plausible. (Of course, the "Imperative Shell" should also be a radically visible interface.)

Here is one idea I was thinking of this morning. Maps and GPS. Imagine trying to get somewhere knowing the name of destination but never really see the map that would include your position and position of your destination with things around and between? Imagine only having GPS that can take you point to point. As a human being what would you choose? Computer will be happy with GPS you provide for it. But you as a human being? Difference between human mind and computer “thinking” is ability and need for higher level of abstraction. So would you choose to have a map or having only GPS is fine? The fact that we stopped using maps in our real travel brought us into scenarios where education is more like having GPS without ever seeing a map.  Higher level of abstractions and creation of “bird views” in my personal opinion are really needed. Tools that allow such view, education materials that create such views. Tutorials, books, films, animation, cartoon books, computer games. “Maps” should be created.   Any coding requires  an Overview first, it helps to get the best path ( algorithm). Teaching programming needs to teach an ability for an overview of things by using an overview of things.

Similar thing in computing will be execution timeline, when you could go back in time, unroll execution.

Yes. in programming terms is watching the data transformed. As watching apples transformed in apple pie cooking. So many times when you are watching apples, you are not watching the crust, amount of sugar, temperature, time. In cooking when you see your crust being raw, you know what to fix because you have an overview what ingredients make a pie and what transformation each ingredient has to make. The tool that allows you to have this overview at all time can help  making a good pie. You can go back into time when you were adding sugar to the apples but if it a temperature and time that really mattered - you are not solving a problem. Good tool that keeps an overview of the pie with states of their ingredients can point out to the problem why your pie is not very good to eat. To make that tool one needs to have an overview. For users of such tool educational materials need to teach to recognize the overview. It is difficult to find resources that can give you an overview of things. I am saying form my own experience as a self-taught. I have to buy a few books, few courses on the same subject because  only going through compartmental explanations in each of them I can build some sense of overview on the subject and only then I can feel that I understand what I am coding.

coding will be needed not just for specialists and solely educated in computer science. although it is up to specialists to create tools and educational materials. I am not such specialist. Not yet. I am just happy to be a part of the group that thinks about the future and direction and I just want to share my view and opinion ( so far) on what I see is happening and what I feel is needed.

Hi <@UBKNXPBAB>

I think many of us here are on-board with the concept of being able to (finally!) see the data that we're "animating" with our functions or behaviours, so :thumbsup::skin-tone-3: to that! And so I like the corresponding switchover from functions to data as the primary entity on the UI, compared with other box-and-line IDEs.

Now, true logic nerds may have something to say here, but to me it seems that this system may not be Turing Complete, because it looks like you only allow pure functions between data items. This, if true, is missing a critical piece of functionality: the ability to remember past states. As I understand it, this would require either recursive function calling, or circular loops, in your diagrams. I would suggest adding circular loops, not recursion, because recursion is hard to understand and visualise.

On the subject raised by <@UCKRZS3DZ> - I/O: you're not far from having it! You do have something rather I/O-like in the non-pure functions that call out to the remote API service and that call a randomisation function.  You also have I/O in the output visualisations: you could imagine building a webpage from rendered forms of output data.  And you can play with example values with the mouse, which is kind of like inputs. All you need is to wire in mouse position, buttons, textfields, etc, as your left-hand values (e.g. button up, button down as a boolean input value) and you have a completely general programming system, assuming you have added the circularity feature!

> Curious if A) you see this as a problem and B) how you might go about solving it.
<@U85MWBKEY> - yes I see this as a *huge* problem. Agree strongly with <@UCUSW7WVD> here. We already had a problem with the repetition in the 3-trier model: db-schema -> [wire schema] -> app server schema -> [wire-schema] -> browser schema. Now this problem has just exploded and continues to grow superlinearly with the number of services involved. The best practices are to make everything optional, check before use, manually chase data flow across services, etc.

I think one key problem is our systems are built with models that make you think 'within the OS process'. When I create a class in Python/C++/Java I'm just defining the fields for an object present locally in memory, for that running instance and manually shuffle fields around via RPCs. The 'greater schema' of your user/data object is an emergent property of the system. Could we start instead from from the greater model and have that represented directly in the system? I think so but it requires throwing away a bunch of existing stuff and moving the ideas of 'database', 'rpc' and 'process' from designed artifacts to generated details.

Hey y'all! Been listening to Steve's podcast for a while now and managed to meet up with him for a bit while he was in Boston. I work at a data pipeline startup, and help run both Boston's Elm Meetup and a weekly functional programming meetup with a few friends.

I also have a future-of-coding-esqe personal project that I've been mapping out for a while, and want to get more serious about implementing. The use case is rapidly developing and combining little personal systems for note-taking, note-organizing, goal tracking, task prioritization, etc. The system itself will be a combination visual + textual editor for a big strongly typed frp-ish program, plus a live-updating view that's a user-defined pure function of the state of the frp network. I'm trying to make it so that "if it compiles, it's deployed". I am in way over my head with this and will need all the help I can get!

The GPS vs map is a fantastic analogy. Much of learning now are guided tutorials on how to accomplish some incremental goal, but they don't really cover the higher level view. It is exactly like following GPS turn-by-turn directions only and then trying to 'think up' what the map looks like. Unfortunately the amount of manufactured complexity one has to wade through is immense.

<@UE6EFEPTQ> Thanks for your thoughts.

Re: Turing-completeness – The system allows recursive function calls. For one example, go to <http://joshuahhh.com/projects/pane/demo/> and press "square roots". There's a function there called "auto-improve guess" which calls itself, to iteratively find a good-enough square root for a number. You can press the little "[+]" to expand out the recursive calls, which is fun, but pretty unwieldy. As you mention, I think providing a visualizable "loop" construct would be more useful, and in particular I think tail recursion is a good foundation for this.

Re: I/O – The function which calls out to an API is a total hack, and if it had an actual side-effect, or depended on sequencing, rather than being an quasi-pure function, its behavior would be totally unpredictable. Anyway, my present stance is that PANE just isn't suited to making stateful, interactive systems, since it doesn't have visualization schemes (like timelines!) suited to seeing the behavior of stateful, interactive systems. You could try to shoehorn them in, but I think you're better off making a new interface for that kind of stuff.

Right, I'll try to ease forwards from Pane to my Onex-tinted world view..
Right, I'll try to ease forwards from Pane to my Onex-tinted world view..

Turing completeness and loops

The pure FP solution that Pane would naturally fall into is what I see as plate-spinning: keep the functions calling and pass evolving, transformed state down and down or round and round, probably like you say with tail recursion, stream-based data types and/or Monads (whack me gently if I'm getting my Computer Science wrong here..). This has the problem that to visualise the nested state becomes very hard, as you said and as you showed in the square root example (I don't see how tail recursion really helps there, but I'd like to see your thoughts).

The not-fully-pure-FP solution (e.g. Onex) allows state as a first class concept and keeps the state "up at the top" where you can visualise it more easily. It can do this by allowing looping or circularity. Instead of the result of a pure function application (or the arguments of a recursive call) being new, transient, anonymous states in the call stack, your pure functions can take top-level first class state values as input, and produce new values as output, back in the top-level state they're associated with.

This makes visualisation much easier - and state chunks, being longer-lived and stable but evolving, can now be given unique identifiers for reference by other chunks.

This has the problem that to visualise the nested state becomes very hard, as you said and as you showed in the square root example (I don't see how tail recursion really helps there, but I'd like to see your thoughts).

The not-fully-pure-FP solution (e.g. Onex) allows state as a first class concept and keeps the state "up at the top" where you can visualise it more easily. It can do this by allowing looping or circularity. Instead of the result of a pure function application (or the arguments of a recursive call) being new, transient, anonymous states in the call stack, your pure functions can take top-level first class state values as input, and produce new values as output, back in the top-level state they're associated with.

This makes visualisation much easier - and state chunks, being longer-lived and stable but evolving, can now be given unique identifiers for reference by other chunks.

I/O

Pretty much falls out of the above approach: some data chunks take in values from the real world, some output what values they have.

I agree. But the only ways I know of  to get any sort of visualization of how a program works are:

1. Read the code and build a diagram yourself with <http://Draw.io|Draw.io> or something like that. Ths is a very slow, manual process, but works well.

2. Use a debugger. The Chrome devtools are the best. You can use devtools to debug non-browser-based Javascript as well. Works well for… well, debugging (viewing all the state around a certain line of code).

3. Dumping a bunch of info to log files and reading through those. Lately I’ve been experimenting with a better way of doing this. I just pushed this to GitHub a few minutes ago:  <https://github.com/JesseAldridge/varlog> The idea is you add a few print statements to your code, dump the output to a normal-looking log file, and then convert that log file into a spreadsheet which gives you an overview of how variables change over time. My hunch is this project will have a high cost/benefit ratio in terms of development time — it’s pretty easy to implement and seeing a spreadsheet full of variables changing over time is a very powerful way to understand what code is doing.

3. Dumping a bunch of info to log files and reading through those. Lately I’ve been experimenting with a better way of doing this. I just pushed this to GitHub a few minutes ago:  <https://github.com/JesseAldridge/varlog> The idea is you add a few print statements to your code, dump the output to a normal-looking log file, and then convert that log file into a spreadsheet which gives you an overview of how variables change over time. My hunch is this project will have a high cost/benefit ratio in terms of development time — it’s pretty easy to implement and seeing a spreadsheet full of variables changing over time is a very powerful way to understand what code is doing.

I think that may be more of a cultural problem than a technical one. Technical topics are always going to be difficult to understand. Our animal minds are naturally built for socialization rather than analytical thinking. We are naturally repelled from thinking difficult thoughts.  <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cognitive_miser>

attachments.title: Cognitive miser
attachments.text: In psychology, the human mind is considered to be a cognitive miser due to the tendency of people to think and solve problems in simpler and less effortful ways rather than in more sophisticated and more effortful ways, regardless of intelligence. Just as a miser seeks to avoid spending money, the human mind often seeks to avoid spending computational effort. The cognitive miser theory is an umbrella theory of cognition that brings together previous research on heuristics and attributional biases to explain how and why people are cognitive misers.The term cognitive miser was first introduced by Susan Fiske and Shelley Taylor in 1984. It is an important concept in social cognition theory and has been influential in other social sciences including but not exclusive to economics and political science.
